labReports.title: Diabetes and sugar review - 2026-06-20
labReports.text: Report date: 2026-06-20 Fasting sample HbA1c 8.2 % (4.0-5.6) Fasting glucose 156 mg/dL (70-99) Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (0.6-1.3) eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m2 (>60) Urine albumin creatinine ratio 38 mg/g (<30) Previous HbA1c 7.6 % Medicines: metformin

markers.label: HbA1c
markers.meaning: Average blood sugar pattern over the past few months.
markers.detail: Higher diabetes-related sugar exposure; review treatment plan, food pattern, activity, and follow-up timing.

markers.label: Fasting glucose
markers.meaning: Blood sugar after fasting.
markers.detail: High fasting sugar; review diabetes plan, meal timing, medicines, and repeat testing.

markers.label: Creatinine
markers.meaning: Kidney-related waste marker; interpretation depends on age, muscle mass, hydration, medicines, and eGFR.
markers.detail: Often within broad adult range; eGFR gives better kidney context.

markers.label: eGFR
markers.meaning: Estimated kidney filtering function.
markers.detail: Usually reassuring if stable and urine findings are acceptable.

markers.label: Urine albumin/creatinine ratio
markers.meaning: Urine marker that helps assess kidney and blood-vessel stress, especially with diabetes or hypertension.
markers.detail: Higher urine albumin needs follow-up with BP, diabetes control, kidney medicines, infection, exercise, and repeat testing context.

labReports.title: QA kidney panel - 2026-06-20
labReports.text: Patient: Naveed QA Report date: 2026-06-20 Panel: Kidney follow-up Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (0.6-1.3) Blood urea nitrogen 28 mg/dL (7-20) eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m2 (>60) Potassium 5.5 mmol/L (3.5-5.1) Sodium 136 mmol/L (135-145) Urine protein positive Symptoms: BP and diabetes follow-up Medicines: BP medicine

markers.label: eGFR
markers.meaning: Estimated kidney filtering function.
markers.detail: Lower eGFR needs follow-up, trend review, urine protein review, medicine review, BP and diabetes control check.

markers.label: Blood urea nitrogen
markers.meaning: Kidney and hydration-related waste marker; interpreted with creatinine, eGFR, fluids, protein intake, and medicines.
markers.detail: High BUN can reflect dehydration, kidney stress, bleeding, protein intake, or medicine context.

markers.label: Creatinine
markers.meaning: Kidney-related waste marker; interpretation depends on age, muscle mass, hydration, medicines, and eGFR.
markers.detail: Higher kidney marker; discuss hydration, medicines, blood pressure, diabetes, and whether repeat testing is needed.

markers.label: Potassium
markers.meaning: Electrolyte important for heart rhythm and muscle function.
markers.detail: High potassium needs prompt clinical review, especially with kidney disease, heart medicines, or weakness.

markers.label: Sodium
markers.meaning: Electrolyte related to fluid balance and brain/nerve function.
markers.detail: Often within common range.

labReports.title: QA CBC fatigue - 2026-06-20
labReports.text: Patient: Naveed QA Report date: 2026-06-20 Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (12-17) Hematocrit 33 % (36-50) RBC 3.9 10^6/uL (4.2-5.9) WBC 12.4 10^3/uL (4-11) Platelets 430 10^3/uL (150-450) MCV 76 fL (80-100) Ferritin 14 ng/mL (30-300) Symptoms: tiredness

markers.label: Ferritin
markers.meaning: Iron storage marker; interpretation changes with inflammation, infection, liver disease, and sex.
markers.detail: Low ferritin can support iron-deficiency context; discuss diet, bleeding, periods, gut symptoms, or repeat testing.

markers.label: Hematocrit
markers.meaning: Percent of blood volume made up of red blood cells; reviewed with hemoglobin and hydration context.
markers.detail: Low hematocrit can fit anemia or blood-loss context and needs review with symptoms and other CBC values.

markers.label: Hemoglobin
markers.meaning: Oxygen-carrying blood protein; low values may suggest anemia context.
markers.detail: Low hemoglobin needs clinician review, especially with fatigue, breathlessness, bleeding, pregnancy, kidney disease, or chest symptoms.

markers.label: MCV
markers.meaning: Average red blood cell size; helps frame anemia patterns with hemoglobin, ferritin, B12, and folate.
markers.detail: Lower MCV can fit iron-deficiency or other small-cell anemia patterns; review ferritin and bleeding context.

markers.label: RBC
markers.meaning: Red blood cell count; interpreted with hemoglobin, hematocrit, MCV, iron, B12, and symptoms.
markers.detail: Low RBC can support anemia context and should be reviewed with hemoglobin, iron, B12, kidney, and bleeding context.

markers.label: WBC
markers.meaning: White blood cell count; can change with infection, inflammation, medicines, and other conditions.
markers.detail: High WBC may reflect infection, inflammation, stress response, medicines, or other causes.

markers.label: Platelets
markers.meaning: Cells involved in clotting.
markers.detail: Often within common adult range.

labReports.title: QA lipid panel - 2026-06-20
labReports.text: Patient: Naveed QA Report date: 2026-06-20 Panel: Lipid profile Fasting sample Total cholesterol 245 mg/dL (<200) LDL 160 mg/dL (<100) HDL 38 mg/dL (>40) Triglycerides 210 mg/dL (<150) Non-HDL cholesterol 207 mg/dL (<130) Symptoms: heart-risk review, routine prevention visit Medicines: statin not listed

markers.label: LDL
markers.meaning: Often called bad cholesterol; lower targets may apply for diabetes or heart disease risk.
markers.detail: Higher heart-risk marker; ask whether lifestyle, medicine review, or tighter targets are needed.

markers.label: Non-HDL cholesterol
markers.meaning: Cholesterol estimate that includes LDL and other atherogenic particles; useful when triglycerides are high.
markers.detail: Higher non-HDL can add heart-risk context; review full cardiovascular risk and lipid plan with a clinician.

markers.label: Total cholesterol
markers.meaning: Overall cholesterol number; risk review depends heavily on LDL, HDL, triglycerides, diabetes, BP, smoking, and age.
markers.detail: Higher heart-risk marker; discuss full lipid profile and prevention plan.

markers.label: Triglycerides
markers.meaning: Blood fat influenced by food pattern, sugar control, alcohol, weight, genetics, and medicines.
markers.detail: Elevated triglycerides; discuss fasting status, sugar control, food pattern, alcohol, and repeat testing.

markers.label: HDL
markers.meaning: Often called good cholesterol; low HDL can add heart-risk context.
markers.detail: Often called good cholesterol; low HDL can add heart-risk context.

medicineEntries.name: Losartan
medicineEntries.dose: Take one tablet every evening as written on label
medicineEntries.concern: I missed one losartan dose and also used ibuprofen for back pain. Build safe pharmacist questions about kidney risk, blood pressure follow-up, and what warning signs I should watch. Safety Context OTC/supplements: Ibuprofen, vitamin D Body context: Kidney condition Storage: Stored as label says High-alert flags: Kidney, liver, transplant, pregnancy Watch Items - severe dizziness - fainting - very low urine Questions To Ask - What should I do after a missed dose if my next dose is due soon? - Should I avoid ibuprofen or ask about kidney-safe alternatives? - What symptoms mean I should call a clinician urgently? Readiness: 95% Boundary: This packet organizes medicine safety questions. It does not prescribe, calculate dosage, change treatment, or replace the prescription label, pharmacist, or clinician.

medicineEntries.name: Amlodipine
medicineEntries.dose: Take one tablet daily as written on label
medicineEntries.concern: I notice ankle swelling and light dizziness after standing. Build pharmacist questions, side-effect precautions, and monitoring points without changing my dose. Safety Context OTC/supplements: Occasional ibuprofen Body context: Older adult / fall risk Storage: Stored as label says High-alert flags: none selected Watch Items - severe dizziness - fainting - rapid swelling Questions To Ask - Could the swelling or dizziness be a known amlodipine effect? - What blood pressure checks should I bring to the next review? - When does swelling mean I should call a clinician urgently? Readiness: 96% Boundary: This packet organizes medicine safety questions. It does not prescribe, calculate dosage, change treatment, or replace the prescription label, pharmacist, or clinician.
